perturbation.original_text: Kennedy acknowledges that the restrictions contained in this Section are reasonable and necessary to protect the legitimate interests of the Company. In the event that any covenant contained in this Section should ever be adjudicated to exceed the time, geographic, product, or service, or other limitations permitted by applicable law in any jurisdiction, then any court is expressly empowered to reform such covenant, and such covenant shall be deemed reformed, in such jurisdiction to the maximum time, geographic, product or service, or other limitations permitted by applicable law.
perturbation.changed_text: Kennedy acknowledges that the restrictions contained in this Section are reasonable and necessary to protect the interests of the Company. In the event that any covenant contained in this Section should ever be adjudicated to exceed the time, geographic limitations, then any arbitrator is expressly empowered to reform such covenant, and such covenant shall be deemed reformed to the maximum time, geographic limitations.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'product or service' limitations, and replacing court with arbitrator, the clause becomes less enforceable. Restricting only time and geographic reach might not adequately protect the company's business interests if Kennedy can still compete using different products or services. Enforceability is further challenged since arbitrators are not held to the same standards as Courts, and their judgement may not take into account the local, state, and federal statutory laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UTSA protects against misappropriation of trade secrets, which can involve specific products or services. Removing the reference to 'product or service' limitations weakens the company's ability to protect its trade secrets, as Kennedy could potentially develop competing products or services without violating the reformed covenant, even if it uses trade secrets to do so. The clause says an arbitrator will be empowered, yet section 2(a) of the UTSA specifies an injunction against a person to prevent them from trade secret misappropriation.

perturbation.justification: The changed text weakens the company's ability to protect its legitimate business interests and trade secrets by removing 'product or service' limitations. The Law Explanation states that 'Removing the reference to 'product or service' limitations weakens the company's ability to protect its trade secrets, as Kennedy could potentially develop competing products or services without violating the reformed covenant, even if it uses trade secrets to do so.' This aligns with the Perturbation Explanation, which states that restricting only time and geographic reach might not adequately protect the company's business interests if Kennedy can still compete using different products or services. The scraped snippets don't directly support this, but the Law and Perturbation explanations directly indicate that trade secrets could be compromised.

perturbation.original_text: Except as may otherwise be required by law, Kennedy agrees not to, without the Company’s prior written consent, (i) disclose or transfer any Proprietary Information to any Person other than the Company or (ii) use any Proprietary Information for any unauthorized purpose, either during the term of this Agreement or the Restricted Period, unless and until such Proprietary Information has become available to the public generally without fault by Kennedy.
perturbation.changed_text: Kennedy agrees not to, without the Company’s prior written consent, (i) disclose or transfer any Proprietary Information to any Person other than the Company or (ii) use any Proprietary Information for any unauthorized purpose, either during the term of this Agreement or the Restricted Period, unless and until such Proprietary Information has become available to the public.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'without fault by Kennedy', Kennedy can disclose proprietary information and claim it became public, regardless of his involvement in its release. This weakens the non-disclosure covenant and opens it to potential legal challenges.
perturbation.law_explanation: The DTSA protects trade secrets from misappropriation. By removing the 'without fault' clause, the agreement fails to adequately protect against situations where Kennedy deliberately or negligently causes the proprietary information to become public, thus weakening the protections intended by the DTSA.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 18 U.S. Code § 1836 - Civil proceedings | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 18 U.S. Code § 1836 - Civil proceedings U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) The Attorney General may, in a civil action, obtain appropriate injunctive relief against any violation of this chapter. (b) Private Civil Actions.— (1) In general.— An owner of a trade secret that is misappropriated may bring a civil action under this subsection if the trade secret is related to a product or service used in, or intended for use in, interstate or foreign commerce. (2) Civil seizure.— (A) In general.— (i) Application.— Based on an affidavit or verified complaint satisfying the requirements of this paragraph, the court may, upon ex parte application but only in extraordinary circumstances, issue an order providing for the seizure of property necessary to prevent the propagation or dissemination of the trade secret that is the subject of the action. (ii) Requirements for issuing order.— The court may not grant an application under clause (i) unless the court finds that it clearly appears from specific facts that— (I) an order issued pursuant to Rule 65 of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure or another form of equitable relief would be inadequate to achieve the purpose of this paragraph because the party to which the order would be issued would evade, avoid, or otherwise not comply with such an order; (II) an immediate and irreparable injury will occur if such seizure is not ordered; (III) the harm to the applicant of denying the application outweighs the harm to the legitimate interests of the person against whom seizure would be ordered of granting the application and substantially outweighs the harm to any third parties who may be harmed by such seizure; (IV) the applicant is likely to succeed in showing that— (aa) the information is a trade secret ; and (bb) the person against whom seizure would be ordered— (AA

perturbation.justification: The modified contract removes the 'without fault by Kennedy' clause. This creates a contradiction because it allows Kennedy to potentially disclose proprietary information and then claim it became public, regardless of his actions that led to its release. The Law Explanation states that this weakens the protections intended by the DTSA. Although Scraped Law Snippet 2 is not helpful, Scraped Law Snippet 1 discusses misappropriation of trade secrets. By removing the 'without fault' clause, the contract no longer adequately protects the company from misappropriation by Kennedy.

perturbation.original_text: Kennedy hereby represents that, except as he has disclosed in writing to Company, Kennedy is not bound by the terms of any agreement with any previous employer or other party to refrain from (i) competing, directly or indirectly, with the business of such previous employer or any other party or (ii) using or disclosing any trade secret or confidential or proprietary information in the course of the Restricted Period. Kennedy further represents that his performance of all the terms of this Agreement does not and will not breach any agreement to keep in confidence proprietary information, knowledge, or data acquired by Kennedy in confidence or in trust prior to the date of the Restricted Period, and Kennedy will not disclose to the Company or induce the Company to use any confidential or proprietary information or material belonging to any previous employer or others.
perturbation.changed_text: Kennedy hereby represents that Kennedy is not bound by the terms of any agreement with any previous employer or other party to refrain from (i) competing, directly or indirectly, with the business of such previous employer or any other party or (ii) using or disclosing any trade secret or confidential or proprietary information. Kennedy further represents that his performance of all the terms of this Agreement will not breach any agreement to keep in confidence proprietary information, knowledge, or data acquired by Kennedy in confidence or in trust, and Kennedy will not disclose to the Company or induce the Company to use any confidential or proprietary information or material belonging to any previous employer or others.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'except as he has disclosed in writing to Company' significantly alters Kennedy's liability. Without this clause, Kennedy is representing that *absolutely* no prior agreements exist, regardless of disclosure. If he *has* disclosed prior agreements, this representation is now false, potentially invalidating the entire agreement. The phrase 'in the course of the Restricted Period' has also been removed to create further ambiguity.
perturbation.law_explanation: This section deals with misrepresentation. Kennedy's representation, now lacking the disclosure exception, becomes a guarantee of no conflicting prior agreements. If this is false, the Company may have grounds to void the agreement based on misrepresentation, even if Kennedy acted in good faith but forgot a minor detail of a prior agreement. By removing the timeframe in which he cannot use or disclose information, it suggests he can't use or disclose information that he is supposed to disclose, which does not make sense.
perturbation.justification: The modification removes the phrase 'except as he has disclosed in writing to Company'. This creates a scenario where Kennedy's representation becomes an absolute guarantee of no conflicting prior agreements, regardless of whether he has already disclosed them. If Kennedy has disclosed prior agreements, this representation is now false, potentially giving the Company grounds to void the agreement based on misrepresentation. The scraped snippets are not useful, but this determination is based on the Law Explanation.

perturbation.original_text: In the event any suit or proceeding against Kennedy to enforce any of the provisions of this Agreement or on account of any damages sustained (or alleged to have been sustained) is brought by the Company by reason of Kennedy’s violation or alleged violation of any of the provisions of this Agreement, the parties agree that, in addition to other costs and damages, all reasonable costs and attorneys’ fees incurred by the party prevailing in such action shall be paid by the other party.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event any suit or proceeding against Kennedy to enforce any of the provisions of this Agreement is brought by the Company by reason of Kennedy’s violation of any of the provisions of this Agreement, the parties agree that, in addition to other costs and damages, all costs and attorneys’ fees incurred by the party prevailing in such action shall be paid by the other party.
perturbation.explanation: Removing "or on account of any damages sustained (or alleged to have been sustained)" and "or alleged violation" implies that the attorney fee shifting only applies if a violation is *proven*, not just *alleged*. Removing "reasonable" makes it so potentially inflated attorney fees can be charged.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Delaware law regarding contract interpretation and attorney's fees (principles of good faith and fair dealing)
perturbation.law_explanation: While Delaware law allows for contractual agreements regarding attorney's fees, it also implies a covenant of good faith and fair dealing in every contract. By removing the 'alleged to have been sustained' and "alleged violation" clauses, there may be an implication that the company is not liable for damages if an alleged breach has no merit. Removing "reasonable" and not requiring the company to pay for Kennedy's legal defense if it brings a frivolous lawsuit against Kennedy and it is found in Kennedy's favor might be seen as acting in bad faith.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Delaware Code Online Title 6 > Chapter 18 Authenticated PDF Â§ 18-1101 Â§ 18-1102 Â§ 18-1103 Â§ 18-1104 Â§ 18-1105 Â§ 18-1106 Â§ 18-1107 Â§ 18-1108 Â§ 18-1109 Â§ 18-1110 TITLE 6 Commerce and Trade SUBTITLE II Other Laws Relating to Commerce and Trade CHAPTER 18. Limited Liability Company Act Subchapter XI. Miscellaneous Â§ 18-1101. Construction and application of chapter and limited liability company agreement. (a) The rule that statutes in derogation of the common law are to be strictly construed shall have no application to this chapter. (b) It is the policy of this chapter to give the maximum effect to the principle of freedom of contract and to the enforceability of limited liability company agreements. (c) To the extent that, at law or in equity, a member or manager or other person has duties (including fiduciary duties) to a limited liability company or to another member or manager or to another person that is a party to or is otherwise bound by a limited liability company agreement, the memberâs or managerâs or other personâs duties may be expanded or restricted or eliminated by provisions in the limited liability company agreement; provided, that the limited liability company agreement may not eliminate the implied contractual covenant of good faith and fair dealing. (d) Unless otherwise provided in a limited liability company agreement, a member or manager or other person shall not be liable to a limited liability company or to another member or manager or to another person that is a party to or is otherwise bound by a limited liability company agreement for breach of fiduciary duty for the memberâs or managerâs or other personâs good faith reliance on the provisions of the limited liability company agreement. (e) A limited liability company agreement may provide for the limitation or elimination of any and all liabilities for breach of contract and breach of duties (including fiduciary duties) of a member, manager or other person to a limited liability company or to another member or manager or to another person

(c) To the extent that, at law or in equity, a member or manager or other person has duties (including fiduciary duties) to a limited liability company or to another member or manager or to another person that is a party to or is otherwise bound by a limited liability company agreement, the memberâs or managerâs or other personâs duties may be expanded or restricted or eliminated by provisions in the limited liability company agreement; provided, that the limited liability company agreement may not eliminate the implied contractual covenant of good faith and fair dealing. (e) A limited liability company agreement may provide for the limitation or elimination of any and all liabilities for breach of contract and breach of duties (including fiduciary duties) of a member, manager or other person to a limited liability company or to another member or manager or to another person
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text weakens the protection offered to Kennedy compared to the original text, creating a potential contradiction with the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing. By removing "or on account of any damages sustained (or alleged to have been sustained)" and "or alleged violation", the modified contract suggests that Kennedy is only protected from attorney fee shifting if an actual, proven violation occurred. The law explanation points out that removing "reasonable" could also be seen as acting in bad faith. Scraped Law Snippet 1 and 2 both state that a limited liability company agreement may not eliminate the implied contractual covenant of good faith and fair dealing. Because these changes could be interpreted as the company acting in bad faith, a contradiction exists.
